About: It is about Azuchi Castle and its nearby town.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the Tenshō Embassy.
It is associated with Alessandro Valignano.
It is associated with Pope Gregory XIII.
It is associated with Oda Nobunaga.
It is associated with Kanō Eitoku.
It is associated with Philips van Winghe.
It is associated with Filippo Ferroverde.
It is associated with Lorenzo Pignoria.
It is associated with Vincent Catari.
It is associated with the Azuchi Screens Research Network.
ComposedBy: It was composed by Kanō Eitoku.
ConsistsOf: It consists of a pair of six-panel folding-screens.
CreatedBy: It was created by Kanō Eitoku.
CreatedFor: It was created for Alessandro Valignano.
HasA: It has a meticulously detailed birds-eye view of the fortress and its nearby town.
HeldIn: It was held in the Vatican collections.
It was held in a gallery of the Vatican.
It was held in the Galleria delle carte geografiche.
It was held in the gallery of Cosmography.
It was held in the court of Madrid.
Influenced: It influenced the development of Japanese folding screens.
IsA: It is a pair of six-panel folding-screens.
It is a seminal work in the development of Japanese folding screens.
KnownAs: It is known as Azuchiyama screens.
It is known as Azuchi Castle screens.
OccurredDuring: It occurred during the Tenshō embassy of 1582–1592.
OccurredIn: It occurred in the Papal apartments of the Vatican.
PartOf: It is part of the Tenshō embassy.
PublishedAs: It was published as woodblock print copies for Lorenzo Pignoria’s addendum.
PublishedIn: It was published in the 1624, 1626, and 1647 editions of Vincent Catari’s Images of the Gods and Ancients.
UsedIn: It is used in studies on the Azuchi castle.
